Economics Graduate School Resources

A guide for undergraduates considering an economics PhD, predoc work, research careers, and the professional economics job market.

← Back to Office Hours & Student Information · Economics Department Information

Seriously, Consider It!

An economics PhD is not “more undergraduate economics.” It is a long research apprenticeship in theory, econometrics, data, institutions, and original intellectual production. It is demanding, hierarchical, and sometimes absurd. It is also one of the few graduate paths where strong students are often funded rather than charged tuition, and it can lead to academic, policy, government, nonprofit, central bank, consulting, technology, finance, and research careers.

Start Here Is This for You? Funding and Commitment Preparation Timeline The Market Resource Library FAQ

Start Here: What an Econ PhD Actually Is

It is a research degree. The goal is not to be a better undergraduate student. The goal is to become someone who can produce new economic knowledge.

The degree

Usually 5–6 years. The first years are coursework and exams. The later years are research, field specialization, seminars, teaching/research assistant work, and a dissertation built around original papers.

ResearchLong horizon

The surprise

Competitive economics PhD programs are often funded. That usually means tuition remission plus a stipend, often in exchange for teaching or research work after the initial fellowship period.

Do not self-fund casually

The signal

Applications are not just “I like economics.” You need evidence: math, statistics/econometrics, programming, research experience, serious writing, strong letters, and proof that you can become a researcher.

Evidence beats vibes

Is This Path for You?

The right question is not “am I smart enough?” It is “do I want the life and work of research enough to train seriously for it?”

Good signs

  • You like hard questions more than easy credentials.
  • You are comfortable with math, statistics, coding, and writing — or willing to get much better.
  • You want to know why a result is true, not merely what the answer is.
  • You can work independently for long periods without constant praise.
  • You have a research question, field, paper, data problem, or model that keeps bothering you.

Warning signs

  • You mainly want a credential.
  • You hate open-ended work.
  • You want guaranteed status, location, or job security.
  • You are allergic to math or econometrics.
  • You are not willing to spend years becoming good before anyone is impressed.

Funding and Commitment

Most serious economics PhD conversations should start from this premise: do not pay sticker price for a standard economics PhD unless you have a very unusual reason.

What funding usually means

Tuition remission, a living stipend, and sometimes health insurance or health-fee support. Funding can be fellowship-based, teaching-assistant-based, research-assistant-based, or some combination.

What you give up

The true cost is opportunity cost: 5–6 years of foregone full-time earnings, geographic flexibility, uncertainty, and delayed entry into other careers.

What you get

Deep research training, access to high-skill labor markets, and the possibility of academic, policy, central-bank, tech, consulting, finance, nonprofit, and government research careers.

Rule of thumb: If a PhD program admits you without meaningful funding, treat that as information. Ask hard questions before accepting.

Preparation: What to Build Before Applying

Admissions committees are trying to predict research success. Give them evidence.

Math

Calculus, linear algebra, probability/statistics, and ideally real analysis. Math is both tool and signal.

Econometrics and data

Take statistics and econometrics seriously. Learn causal inference, robustness, replication, and the limits of identification.

Programming

Python, R, Stata, SQL, data cleaning, Git, reproducible workflows, and comfort with messy data.

Research

RA work, thesis/capstone, independent papers, replication projects, predoc work, or substantial faculty-supervised research.

Letters

You need economists or serious quantitative researchers who can credibly say you can do research.

Writing

Write clearly. A statement of purpose should show fields, questions, papers, data, and fit — not generic enthusiasm.

Taste

Read papers until you can tell the difference between clever, important, fashionable, and empty.

Grit

Graduate school rewards persistence, independence, and the ability to recover from rejection, confusion, and slow progress.

A Sensible Undergraduate Timeline

This is not lockstep. It is a way to avoid arriving in senior year with vague interest and no evidence.

First year
  • Take economics seriously, but do not mistake principles courses for graduate economics.
  • Start calculus and statistics preparation early.
  • Talk to faculty if you find yourself wanting more depth.
Second year
  • Take intermediate micro/macro and statistics/econometrics/data methods.
  • Add linear algebra, probability, programming, and serious writing.
  • Begin RA work or an independent empirical/theoretical project if possible.
Third year
  • Take advanced electives and methods courses.
  • Develop a thesis, capstone, RA project, or serious writing sample.
  • Decide whether you are applying directly, pursuing a predoc, or building more preparation first.
Summer before senior year
  • Draft a program list and application plan.
  • Ask potential letter writers what evidence they would need to write strongly.
  • Prepare a CV, statement draft, transcript, writing sample, and research summary.
Senior year
  • Apply to a realistic distribution of programs or predoc/RA positions.
  • Keep doing research and serious coursework after applications are submitted.
  • Remember: “not yet” is not “never.” A predoc can be the right bridge.

The Econ PhD Market: What Students Should See Early

The market is broad, data-rich, and hierarchical. Students should understand both the opportunities and the structure before committing.

Figure shared in the material for this page: flows from PhD programs to departments by rank category. The main student lesson is not despair; it is realism. Placement is hierarchical, but not zero-sum in the way undergraduates often imagine. The PhD path includes academic, policy, central bank, research, consulting, finance, technology, and nonprofit tracks.

How to read the market

  • JOE shows what economists are hired to do.
  • Job-market papers show what new PhDs are producing.
  • EconTrack shows timing, interviews, offers, and congestion.
  • Predoc listings show what research skills undergraduates are expected to build.
  • Placement pages reveal hierarchy, advisor networks, and field demand.
The job market changes. Students should not make life decisions from one year’s panic or one year’s boom. But they should learn the market before entering it.

Searchable Resource Library

Search by topic: funding, math, predoc, applications, statement, job market, JOE, AEA, Marginal Revolution, letters, research, writing, forums, or careers.

Showing all resources.

Start Here

AEA: Preparing for Graduate School

The central AEA student portal for graduate-school preparation: degrees, timeline, funding, math preparation, rankings, job market, and doctoral-career pathways.

Applications

AEA: Suggested Application Timeline

A practical planning sequence for building a list of programs, gathering letters, preparing materials, and applying on time.

Preparation

AEA: Math Preparation for Graduate School

The clearest official reminder that serious economics graduate preparation is also serious math preparation.

Funding

AEA: Funding Opportunities for Graduate School

Useful overview of how graduate school is financed and where students can look for support beyond program funding.

Programs

AEA: US Graduate Programs in Economics

Alphabetical list of US graduate programs in economics and related degrees. Start here when building a program list.

Careers

AEA: Careers in Economics

A broad AEA guide to where economics training leads: business, law, government, nonprofits, international relations, and academia.

Careers

AEA: The Economics Profession

Overview of research and teaching careers for economists, including academic and nonacademic research work.

Job Market

AEA: JOE Network

The official American Economic Association job market hub for economists. Students should browse it to understand what PhD-level jobs look like.

Job Market

AEA: EconTrack Job Market Information Board

Tracks job-market status information such as interview invitations, flyouts, offers, and closed searches.

Job Market

AEA: Committee on the Job Market

AEA committee resources and updates on the economics PhD job market.

Job Market

AEA: Job Market Guide and Advice by John Cawley

Professional guide to the economics PhD job market process, especially useful for understanding what students eventually face.

Predoc / RA

PREDOC: Opportunities

Central listing of predoctoral research positions. These jobs are now a major bridge from undergraduate study to competitive PhD applications.

Predoc / RA

PREDOC: Education Programs

Programs and training that help students learn about predoctoral work, coding, research skills, and doctoral-career pathways.

Predoc / RA

NBER: Research Assistant Positions — not at NBER

Frequently updated list of full-time research positions connected to NBER-affiliated researchers, often lasting 1–3 years.

Applications

Economics PhD Application Guide

A student-friendly guide to applying to economics PhD programs, including preparation, applications, and what admissions committees are looking for.

Applications

Susan Athey: Advice for Applying to Grad School in Economics

Classic advice from Susan Athey on interests, preparation, essays, letters, and how to think about economics PhD applications.

Applications

Jesse Shapiro: Notes on Applying for a PhD in Economics

Concise notes on the key elements of an economics PhD application.

Applications

Chris Blattman: PhD Applicants and Statement of Purpose

Especially useful for turning vague interests into a credible statement of purpose.

Applications

Chris Blattman: FAQs on PhD Applications

Blunt, useful answers about research experience, field choice, and whether a PhD is the right move.

Grad School Life

Greg Mankiw: Advice for Grad Students

Mankiw collects classic advice from senior economists on research topics, writing papers, finishing the PhD, job market preparation, publishing, and talks.

Preparation

Greg Mankiw: Why Aspiring Economists Need Math

A simple explanation of why admissions committees care so much about math: theory, econometrics, reading the literature, rigor, and signaling.

Research Craft

Masayuki Kudamatsu: Tips 4 Economists

Large curated page of advice links for economists: research, writing, presenting, publishing, job markets, and professional survival.

Research Craft

Michael Weisbach: A Young Scholar’s Introduction

A field guide to doing economics: research, professional development, writing, presentations, and becoming a scholar.

Research Craft

John Cochrane: Writing Tips for PhD Students

Advice on how economists should write research papers: clarity, structure, contribution, and discipline.

The Profession

A Fine Theorem: Is Theory Really Dead?

A snapshot of 2013–14 job-market stars and what they were producing; useful for seeing fields, methods, and intellectual fashion.

The Profession

Arpit Gupta: PhD-to-department flows

Visual evidence on hierarchy and placement flows from PhD programs to departments. The figure is included on this page as a cautionary signal.

Forums / Caveats

Economics Job Market Rumors

A notorious forum that can reveal professional anxieties and rumors, but should be used carefully and sparingly. Do not let anonymous forums shape your identity or confidence.

Forums / Caveats

EconSpark Discussion Forum

AEA-hosted professional discussion forum for economists.

Community

Women in Empirical Micro

A useful professional community/account for following empirical microeconomics, seminars, people, and opportunities.

Trackers

Economic Conferences Spreadsheet

A crowdsourced tracker for economics conferences and submission dates.

Trackers

Econ RA / Predoc Spreadsheet

Crowdsourced tracker for economics RA and predoc opportunities.

Grad School Life

PhD Comics

Comic relief. Not a plan for your life, but occasionally more accurate than it should be.

Grad School Life

Life Among the Econ

A famous anthropological spoof of the economics profession. Funny because there is enough truth in it.

The Profession

Capitalism and Freedom: Steven Levitt on the Future of Econ

A skeptical view of whether the profession rewards practical insight or increasingly inward-looking technical difficulty.

Funding

Bryan Caplan: Is the Econ PhD a Free Lunch?

A provocative way to think about funding, opportunity cost, and whether an economics PhD can be unusually cheap relative to other graduate paths.

Research Craft

Bryan Caplan: My Research Program for PhD Students

A proposed research path for PhD students: opinionated, contrarian, and useful for thinking about taste and agenda formation.

The Profession

Bryan Caplan: Economists with Pseudo-Knowledge

A warning that technical skill and professional standing do not automatically equal wisdom.

Books

Stuart Hillmon: Getting a Ph.D. in Economics

Book-length guide to the economics PhD path. Useful for students who want a full narrative rather than a checklist.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: Getting a Ph.D. in Economics

Tyler Cowen’s discussion of Hillmon’s book and the gap between undergraduate economics and graduate economics.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: Graduate School Advice

Cowen’s blunt emphasis on the job-market paper, letters, publications, and the outputs that define academic candidates.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: Against Errands

Cowen/Tabarrok lesson for young scholars: protect attention and do not let low-value errands eat the time needed for reading, writing, and research.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: In Praise of Books

A reminder that economists need deep reading, not only problem sets, code, and papers.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: The Beginnings of My Early Job Publishing

Tyler Cowen’s story of turning undergraduate work into published papers. The lesson: start earlier than you think and do real work now.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: What’s the Hardest Part of Being a PhD Student?

Alex Tabarrok explains the jump from learning existing knowledge to creating new knowledge in a dissertation.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: Advice for Economics Grad Students vis-à-vis AI

A current AI-era warning: students should build complementarities with AI rather than pretend the research production function is unchanged.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: Track the Economics Job Market

Points readers to tools aggregating job-market papers, postings, and conference information.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: Economics Job Market Update

A recent warning that the PhD economist job market has been unusually difficult, with private-sector demand as an important bright spot.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: How the Job Market Works at Top Schools

A useful, uncomfortable look at hierarchy, placement, and the mechanics of top-school job markets.

Marginal Revolution

Marginal Revolution: What is Economics These Days?

A broad reflection on how the boundaries of economics have changed and why math/computer science backgrounds matter.

Mentoring

MIT/Harvard/Berkeley/Princeton/Stanford/Yale EMP / Mentoring

Mentoring resources for students curious about economics PhD paths, especially students with less access to inside information.

Grad School Life

Laura's 15 Rules for Grad School

A practical, student-facing survival guide for graduate school.

No resources match that search. Try a broader term such as “application,” “research,” “funding,” “predoc,” or “job market.”

Economics PhD FAQ for Undergraduates

Short answers to the questions students usually ask too late.

Do I have to know I want to be a professor?

No. Many economics PhDs work outside academia: central banks, government agencies, international organizations, consulting, finance, technology firms, policy institutes, and nonprofits. But the training is still research training, so you need to want the research work itself.

Do I need a predoc?

Not always, but predocs have become increasingly important for competitive applicants, especially students who need more research experience, stronger letters, coding/data credentials, or clearer fields of interest.

What math should I take?

At minimum: calculus, linear algebra, probability/statistics, and econometrics. Stronger applicants often add real analysis, optimization, differential equations, mathematical statistics, or proof-based math. The exact portfolio depends on target programs and fields.

What if I discover this late?

Late is not fatal. But it changes the path. You may need a predoc, additional math, RA work, a master’s program, or a delayed application cycle. The right answer is usually to build evidence rather than rush weak applications.

Should I pay for a master’s first?

Sometimes, but not automatically. A master’s can help if it gives you math, econometrics, research, letters, and a credible transcript signal. It is not valuable merely because it is another credential.

What makes a letter strong?

A strong letter says, with evidence, that you can do research. The best letters usually come from faculty who have seen your independent analytical work, RA work, thesis work, coding/data work, or graduate-level performance.

What should I read to know what economists are studying?

Browse NBER working papers, AEA journals, job-market papers, JOE fields, faculty websites, seminar schedules, EconTrack, and field-specific communities. Look for questions, data, models, identification strategies, and writing style.

Should I read Econ Job Market Rumors?

Maybe once, carefully, to understand that the profession has status anxieties and gossip like every profession. Do not live there. Anonymous forums can badly distort how students understand the field and themselves.
